text: The laparoscopic procedure begins, providing a view of the abdominal cavity and the affected ileal strictures.
The surgeon performs a full small bowel examination, visually inspecting the bowel from the ileocecal junction to the duodenojejunal (DJ) junction.
The surgeon identifies several dense stricturing masses in the mid to distal ileum, with visible gross chronic sacculation of the intervening segments.
The surgeon observes the proximal jejunum, noting its normal appearance without any visible strictures or abnormalities.
The surgeon delivers the diseased segment of the bowel through a short incision by extending the umbilical port site, revealing the affected tissue.
The surgeon observes atonic sacculated segments between strictures in the mid to distal ileum, measuring approximately eleven centimeters in diameter.
The surgeon performs a single resection of the small bowel, removing all identified strictures in the mid to distal ileum, including the sacculated segments.
The surgeon performs a side-to-side stapled anastomosis, connecting the resected bowel segments.
During the relaparoscopy, the surgeon conducts a second examination of the small bowel following the construction of the anastomosis.
